subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the two components of normal uterine position, with their angles?
back: Anteflexion = angle between uterus & cervix (120°); Anteversion = angle between cervix & vagina (90°).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which ligament keeps the uterus in the anteverted position?
back: Round ligament.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which ligament chiefly prevents retroversion of the uterus?
back: Uterosacral ligament (> round ligament).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Name the three ligaments that SUPPORT the uterus.
back: Cardinal (transverse cervical / Mackenrodt's), uterosacral, and pubocervical ligaments.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which ligament does NOT support the uterus?
back: Broad ligament.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which muscle is the main muscular support of the uterus?
back: Levator ani (pubococcygeus + iliococcygeus).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Three structures attached at the fundus of the uterus (mnemonic R-T-O)?
back: Round ligament, fallopian Tube, Ovarian ligament.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which ligament connects the ovary to the uterus, and which connects ovary to pelvic side wall?
back: Ovary→uterus: ovarian ligament. Ovary→pelvic side wall: infundibulopelvic / suspensory ligament of ovary (contains ovarian vessels).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Blood supply of the uterus?
back: Uterine artery (branch of anterior division of internal iliac artery) + utero-ovarian anastomosis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Nerve supply of the uterus (spinal levels)?

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cervix-to-corpus ratio at: birth / before puberty / at puberty / reproductive age / after menopause?

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: The uterus develops from which embryological structure?
back: Mullerian (paramesonephric) duct.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Round ligament vs ovarian ligament develop from which parts of the gubernaculum?
back: Round ligament: distal end. Ovarian ligament: proximal end.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: [DRAW & LABEL] Sketch the uterus with fundal attachments and the broad-ligament contents.
back: Label: round ligament, fallopian tube, ovarian ligament at fundus; uterine & ovarian arteries; ureter crossing under uterine artery ~2 cm lateral to internal os.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Hysteroscopy is used to do what, and gas vs liquid distension media uses?
back: Visualise the uterus from inside. Gas = diagnostic only; liquid = diagnosis + treatment.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which distension media are used with UNIPOLAR cautery? Give examples.
back: Electrolyte-deficient media: glycine (most common), mannitol, sorbitol.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Fluid-deficit cut-off to STOP hysteroscopy: electrolyte-deficient vs electrolyte-rich media?
back: Electrolyte-deficient: 1000 mL. Electrolyte-rich: 2500 mL.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Electrolyte-rich media: which is most commonly used and which is the 'best'?
back: Normal saline = most commonly used; Ringer lactate = best media.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Indications for hysteroscopy?
back: Asherman syndrome, septate uterus, endometrial polypectomy, type 0 & 1 (submucous) fibroids.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Branches of the uterine artery in order?
back: Uterine → arcuate → radial → basal & spiral arteries.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Stepwise devascularization order in PPH management?
back: 1) Uterine artery (U/L then B/L), 2) utero-ovarian anastomosis, 3) anterior division of internal iliac artery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In Trisulnikov's triple ligation, what is the 3rd vessel ligated?
back: Sampson's artery (artery of the round ligament).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the 'living ligature' of the uterus?
back: The middle layer of the myometrium.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the canal of Nuck and its clinical significance?
back: Fold of peritoneum accompanying the round ligament into the inguinal canal; can lead to a hernia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Artery of the round ligament: name and origin?
back: Sampson artery; arises from inferior epigastric artery (→ uterine artery).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Broad ligament contents: 1 tube, 2 ligaments, 2 arteries, 3 remnants — name them.
back: Tube: fallopian tube. Ligaments: round + ovarian. Arteries: uterine + ovarian. Remnants: epoophoron, paroophoron, Gartner's duct.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: The ureter lies posterior to which vessels and anterior to which?
back: Posterior to ovarian & uterine arteries; anterior to the internal iliac artery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Most common site of ureteric injury in gynae surgery?
back: ~2 cm lateral to the internal os ('water under the bridge' — uterine artery crosses the ureter).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Rank hysterectomies by RISK of ureteric injury (highest → lowest).
back: Modified radical/Wertheim's > radical > laparoscopic > total abdominal > vaginal hysterectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Retroverted/retroflexed uterus: what % of women, and is it correctable?
back: ~20%; mobile type can be manually corrected.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Causes of a FIXED retroverted uterus?
back: Endometriosis (most common) > PID / genital TB; cannot be corrected manually.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Order of clamping in abdominal hysterectomy?
back: 1) Round-Tube-Ovarian (RTO) pedicle, 2) cardinal ligament, 3) uterine artery, 4) uterosacral ligament. (Reversed in vaginal hysterectomy.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Total abdominal hysterectomy removes what, and where do you clamp with vs without BSO?
back: Removes uterus + cervix. Without BSO: clamp at ovarian ligament. With BSO: clamp at infundibulopelvic ligament.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Two parts of the cervix with their location and colour?
back: Endocervix (supravaginal, red); exocervix (portio vaginalis, pink).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Epithelial lining of endocervix vs exocervix?
back: Endocervix: columnar epithelium. Exocervix: stratified squamous epithelium.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the transformation zone / squamocolumnar junction, and at what level does it lie?
back: Junction of endo- and exocervix; located at the external os.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Clinical significance of the transformation zone?
back: Most common site for pre-invasive and invasive lesions of the cervix.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Situations in which the transformation zone moves OUT?
back: Puberty, OCP use, pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is cervical ectropion — and key clinical points?
back: Endocervix seen moving onto the exocervix; physiological in pregnancy; do NOT biopsy; can cause post-coital bleeding.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Normal cervical length: non-pregnant vs pregnant?
back: Non-pregnant: 2.5 cm. Pregnant: 4 cm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define 'short cervix' in pregnancy and its risks.
back: Cervical length < 2.5 cm; risk of T2 abortion and preterm labour.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: ACOG cervical-length surveillance: for whom, by what method, and what GA window?
back: For h/o T2 abortion or preterm labour; by TVS; cervical length measured between 16–24 weeks of gestation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Blood supply of the cervix?
back: Descending cervical artery (branch of uterine artery).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Nerve supply of the cervix?

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Lymphatic drainage of the cervix (mnemonic H-O-P-E)?
back: Hypogastric/internal iliac, Obturator, Paracervical, External iliac LNs.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which nodes does the cervix NOT drain into, and what is its sentinel node?
back: Does NOT drain to superficial inguinal LNs; sentinel node = paracervical LN.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Duhrssen's incision: indication and site?
back: For entrapped head in breech; incisions above/below the 3 and 9 o'clock positions (avoiding the artery).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Source and spinal level of pain in 1st vs 2nd stage of labour?
back: 1st stage: uterine contractions (T10–L1). 2nd stage: cervical dilation (S2–S4).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Epidural analgesia for painless labour reaches which level; LSCS anaesthesia which level?
back: Painless labour epidural: T10. LSCS anaesthesia: T4.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Pudendal block: indication and landmark?
back: Instrumental delivery; pierce the sacrospinous ligament at the ischial spine.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Paracervical block: indication and key caution?
back: Suction & evacuation; never inject at 3 and 9 o'clock.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Uterine sound — features and use (vs bladder sound)?
back: Calibrated, olive-tipped; assesses size & position of uterus. Bladder sound: no calibrations.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Vulsellum forceps — features and use?
back: Holds the lip of the NON-pregnant cervix; ratchet lock, curved blade with a gap, rat-like teeth.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which forceps holds the cervix of a PREGNANT woman?
back: Sponge-holding forceps.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Distinguishing feature: Allis vs Kocher's forceps?
back: Allis: no curve. Kocher's: no gap.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Anterior vaginal wall retractor vs blunt-sharp curette — appearance?
back: Ant. vaginal wall retractor: large loop with serrations. Blunt-sharp curette: small loop.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Epithelial lining of the vagina?
back: Stratified non-keratinized squamous epithelium.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Döderlein bacilli — function and lifespan?
back: Lactobacilli that convert glycogen → lactic acid; appear at puberty, disappear at menopause.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Embryological origin of the vagina (upper vs lower)?
back: Upper part: Mullerian duct. Lower part: urogenital sinus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Does the vagina contain glands?
back: No.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Vaginal pH at: birth / childhood / reproductive age / pregnancy / menstruation / menopause?
back: Birth: acidic. Childhood: alkaline (6–8). Reproductive: 4–4.5. Pregnancy: <4. Menstruation: 6–8. Menopause: 6–8.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Predominant vaginal cell in: estrogen excess / progesterone excess / no hormone?
back: Estrogen: superficial cells. Progesterone: intermediate cells. No hormone: parabasal cells.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Two factors responsible for acidic vaginal pH?
back: Estrogen (provides glycogen) + Döderlein bacilli.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When is vaginal defense lost after birth?
back: 10 days after birth.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: pH > 4.5 is associated with which infections; which organism tolerates acidic pH (≤4.5)?
back: >4.5: bacterial vaginosis, trichomoniasis. Tolerates acidic pH: Candida.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Parabasal cells: when seen and microscopic appearance?
back: Menopause, before puberty, immediately after childbirth; blue with hazy outline, big nuclei.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Intermediate cells: when seen and appearance?
back: Secretory phase, pregnancy; blue with small nuclei.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Superficial cells: when seen and appearance?
back: Proliferative phase; eosinophilic (pink) with pyknotic nuclei.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: De Lancey LEVEL 1 — structures, support, and defects produced?
back: Uterosacral & cardinal ligaments; support upper 1/3 vagina; defect → apical/vault prolapse, cervical elongation, enterocele.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: De Lancey LEVEL 2 — structure, support, defects?
back: Arcus tendineus fascia; supports middle 1/3 vagina; defect → cystocele (most common), rectocele.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: De Lancey LEVEL 3 — structure, support, defects?
back: Perineal body & attached muscles; support lower 1/3 vagina; defect → urethrocele, rectocele, lax perineum.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Surgical repair: cystocele/urethrocele vs rectocele/lax perineum?
back: Anterior wall (cystocele/urethrocele): anterior colporrhaphy. Posterior (rectocele/lax perineum): posterior colpoperineorrhaphy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Enterocele repair — preferred and outdated techniques?
back: Preferred: McCall culdoplasty. Outdated: Moschowitz repair, Halban's repair.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Does vaginal prolapse management depend on age and parity?
back: No.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Length and lining of the fallopian tube?
back: 10–12 cm; ciliated columnar epithelium.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Parts of the fallopian tube from medial → lateral?
back: Interstitium → isthmus → ampulla → infundibulum.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Three cell types in the fallopian tube?
back: Secretory cells, ciliated cells, resting (peg) cells.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Nerve supply of the fallopian tube?

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Blood supply of the fallopian tube (medial vs lateral)?
back: Medial 1/3: uterine artery. Lateral 2/3: ovarian artery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Narrowest vs widest part of the tube?
back: Narrowest: interstitium. Widest: ampulla.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Anatomical vs physiological sphincter of the tube?
back: Anatomical sphincter: interstitium. Physiological sphincter: isthmus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Where is tubal ligation performed, and why there?
back: At the isthmus (uniformly thick).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Where is the Essure device inserted?
back: Interstitial/intramural part.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Most common site for fertilisation, ectopic pregnancy, and genital TB in the tube?
back: Ampulla.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Is tubal ligation reversible, and what is its failure rate?
back: Permanent, non-reversible (not a LARC); failure rate <0.5%.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Eligibility for tubal ligation: age, children, marital status, spouse criteria?
back: Age 22–49 yrs; ≥1 child >1 yr old; ever married; spouse need not have had vasectomy; spousal consent NOT required.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Laparoscopic tubal ligation: device used, when it is NOT done, and a relative contraindication?
back: Most common method; uses Fallope rings (silicone + barium sulphate); NOT done for postpartum ligation; relative C/I: severe cardiac/pulmonary disease. Interval ligation done after 6 weeks of delivery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Postpartum tubal ligation: timing, method, and Pomeroy vs modified Pomeroy technique?
back: After 24 hrs to 1 week of delivery; via mini-laparotomy. Pomeroy: single ligature (chromic catgut). Modified Pomeroy: double ligature (plain catgut).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Hysteroscopic tubal ligation: material, when effective, how checked, success rate?
back: Material: nickel & titanium; effective after 3 months (confirm by HSG); success rate 99%.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Referred pain from the ovary is felt where, and why?
back: Medial thigh (via cutaneous branch of the obturator nerve).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Investigation of choice for an adnexal mass?
back: TVS (transvaginal sonography).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Most common cancer type — fallopian tube, uterus, endocervix?
back: Adenocarcinoma.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Most common cancer type — cervix, vagina, vulva?
back: Squamous cell carcinoma.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Vulval cancer sentinel node vs clitoris drainage?
back: Vulva: superficial inguinal LN → deep inguinal (femoral). Clitoris: node of Cloquet / Rosenmuller's node.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which gene determines body sex, and where is it located?
back: SRY gene; on the distal short arm of the Y chromosome.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In the testis: what do Sertoli cells and Leydig cells each produce?
back: Sertoli cells: anti-Müllerian hormone (MIS) + inhibin B (during IUL). Leydig cells: testosterone (first stimulus = hCG).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Role of AMH/MIS vs testosterone in male development?
back: AMH causes Müllerian duct regression; testosterone promotes Wolffian duct development (seminal vesicle, epididymis, ejaculatory duct, vas deferens).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Wolffian (mesonephric) duct remnants in the male?
back: Prostatic utricle, appendix of testis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In the female (no Leydig/Sertoli cells): what happens to the Müllerian and Wolffian ducts?
back: No testosterone → no DHT → female external genitalia; no AMH → Müllerian duct develops (fallopian tube, uterus, cervix, upper vagina); Wolffian duct regresses (remnants: epoophoron, paroophoron, Gartner's duct).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which enzyme converts testosterone to DHT, and what does DHT form?
back: 5α-reductase; DHT → male external genitalia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: External genitalia develop from which structure, and homologues of genital tubercle / fold / swelling?
back: Urogenital sinus. Genital tubercle → penis (M) / clitoris (F). Genital fold → penile urethra / labia minora. Genital swelling → scrotum / labia majora.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Developmental timeline: when do Leydig cells produce testosterone, Müllerian/Wolffian duct regress, and external genitalia differentiate?
back: ~8 wk: Leydig cells make testosterone. ~9 wk: Müllerian/Wolffian duct regression. ~12 wk: external genitalia develop. ~14 wk: sex of fetus can be differentiated.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Bartholin's gland: location, duct opening site, and named cancer criteria?
back: Between labia majora & minora; ducts open into vestibule outside hymen at junction of ant. 2/3 & post. 1/3 (4 & 8 o'clock); cancer named by Honan's criteria.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Vestibule: anterior, posterior, lateral boundaries and the openings within it?
back: Boundaries — ant.: clitoris; post.: fourchette; lateral: labia minora. Openings — Skene's glands, urethra, vagina, Bartholin gland ducts.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Management of a Bartholin's cyst by category?
back: <3 cm asymptomatic: expectant. ≥3 cm asymptomatic: I&D then Word catheter. Symptomatic/abscess: I&D + Word catheter. Recurrent cyst: marsupialization.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: [DRAW & LABEL] Sketch the female external genitalia / vestibule.
back: Label: clitoris (ant.), paraurethral (Skene) glands, urethra, vaginal introitus covered by hymen, Bartholin glands (4 & 8 o'clock), fourchette (post.).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Most common and 2nd most common histological types of vulval cancer?
back: Most common: squamous cell carcinoma. 2nd most common: melanoma > adenocarcinoma.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Vulval cancer: typical age group, most common symptom, and most common site?
back: Age 50–70 yrs (mean 65); most common symptom: pruritus; most common site: labia majora > labia minora.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Two common varieties of squamous vulval cancer?
back: Keratinizing (most common) and warty.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Risk factors for vulval cancer (mnemonic H-P-V-S)?
back: HPV (HPV 16); Paget's disease / p53 mutation; VIN / vulval atrophy / vulval warts (condyloma acuminata); Smoking / lichen sclerosus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which conditions are NOT risk factors for vulval cancer?
back: Condyloma lata, herpes simplex virus, vulval hamartoma.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Vulval cancer staging: Stage IA vs IB; at which stages are lymph nodes involved?
back: Stage IA: size <2 cm AND depth <1 mm. Stage IB: size ≥2 cm OR depth ≥1 mm. LN involved in stage III & IV.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Vulval cancer management by stage (medical/surgical)?
back: Stage 1–2: surgery (excision of tumour + LND). Stage 3–4: chemoradiation. Surgery: Stage IA = simple partial vulvectomy; IB/II = radical vulvectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Lymphatic dissection rules in vulval cancer?
back: Stage IA: no LND. LN palpable or positive: B/L inguinofemoral LND. Default: B/L inguinofemoral LND — except may be U/L only if ALL met: size <2 cm + tumour >2 cm from midline + no local extension.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Müllerian agenesis: which ducts are involved, and what about ovary/ovulation?
back: Both Müllerian ducts absent; ovary normal, ovulation normal.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Unicornuate uterus: defect, and the count of tube/uterus/cervix/upper vagina?
back: One Müllerian duct absent. 1 fallopian tube, ½ uterus, ½ cervix, ½ upper vagina.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: HSG findings in unicornuate uterus?
back: Single fallopian tube, banana-shaped uterus, one Leech-Wilkinson cannula.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Uterus didelphys: defect and counts; what is the distinguishing feature?
back: Both ducts present but fail to fuse (or fuse only in vagina). 2 tubes, 2 uteri, 2 cervices (distinguishing feature), 1 or 2 vaginas; HSG uses 2 Leech-Wilkinson cannulae.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Bicornuate uterus: defect, counts, fundus, and HSG angle/distance?
back: Ducts fuse in cervix & vagina but NOT uterus. 2 tubes, 2 uteri, 1 cervix, 1 vagina; fundus divided. HSG: inter-horn angle obtuse, distance ≥4 cm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Septate uterus: defect, counts, fundus, and HSG angle/distance?
back: Ducts fuse but septum fails to resorb. 2 tubes, 2 uteri, 1 cervix, 1 vagina; fundus undivided. HSG: inter-horn angle acute, distance <4 cm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which two anomalies can HSG NOT differentiate between?
back: Septate vs bicornuate uterus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Arcuate uterus: fundal appearance and reproductive significance?
back: Fundus flat/dimpled; best reproductive outcome.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In-utero DES exposure: most common, most specific anomaly, and most common malignancy?
back: M/c anomaly: hypoplastic uterus. Most specific: T-shaped uterus. M/c malignancy: clear cell cancer of vagina.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Most common uterine anomaly overall?
back: Septate > bicornuate uterus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Anomaly with best vs worst reproductive outcome?
back: Best: arcuate > didelphys. Worst: septate uterus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Associations of unicornuate uterus?
back: Ectopic pregnancy, ectopic ovary, U/L dysmenorrhea, U/L renal anomalies.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Müllerian anomaly most associated with renal anomalies?
back: Unicornuate uterus > Müllerian agenesis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Müllerian anomaly most associated with transverse lie? (trick)
back: Uterine didelphys is the one most commonly NOT associated with transverse lie.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Most common complications of Müllerian malformations (obstetric vs gynaecological)?
back: Obstetric: recurrent abortions. Gynaecological: endometriosis, dysmenorrhea.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Investigations for Müllerian malformations: IOC, gold standard, last resort?
back: IOC: 3D ultrasound. Gold standard: MRI. Last resort: laparoscopy + hysteroscopy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Surgery for Müllerian malformations — indication, and preferred/outdated for septate vs bicornuate/didelphys?
back: Indication: recurrent abortions. Septate — preferred: hysteroscopic septal resection; outdated: Jones metroplasty. Bicornuate/didelphys — preferred: unification surgery; outdated: Straussman metroplasty.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: [DRAW & LABEL] Draw the 6 classic uterine anomalies (agenesis, unicornuate, didelphys, bicornuate, septate, arcuate).
back: Compare fundal contour and tube/uterus/cervix/vagina counts for each.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Three muscle groups supporting the uterus?
back: Levator ani; bulbospongiosus; transverse perinei (superficial & deep).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Mechanical support angles — anteversion vs anteflexion?
back: Anteversion 90°; anteflexion 120°.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the first step in the development of prolapse?
back: Retroversion.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which ligament is the SECONDARY support of the uterus?
back: Round ligament.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which part of levator ani, if damaged, most causes prolapse?
back: Puborectalis > pubococcygeus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: POP-Q classification: what is the reference point?
back: The hymen.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Prolapse degrees relative to the hymen (1st/2nd/3rd)?
back: 1st: >1 cm above hymen. 2nd: within 1 cm of hymen. 3rd: >1 cm below hymen.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define procidentia and vault prolapse.
back: Procidentia: entire cervix & uterus lie outside the hymen. Vault prolapse: prolapse of the vaginal stump after hysterectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Risk factors for prolapse?
back: Elderly/menopausal, birth trauma, multiparity, instrumental delivery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Best method to PREVENT prolapse?
back: Perineal / Kegel exercises.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: POP-Q grid: name the 9 sites measured, and the first measurement / patient maneuver.
back: Aa, Ba (anterior wall); C (cervix/cuff); gh (genital hiatus); pb (perineal body); tvl (total vaginal length); Ap, Bp (posterior wall); Dp (posterior fornix). First measure tvl; taken during Valsalva.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Surgical management of prolapse — vaginal vs abdominal approach?
back: Vaginal: uterosacral suspension (prophylactic & therapeutic), sacrospinous fixation. Abdominal (better outcome, more difficult): sacral colpopexy — mesh from sacral promontory to vault.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: At which degree of prolapse is surgery indicated, and does management depend on age/parity?
back: Surgery only for 3rd-degree prolapse; management depends on age & parity.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Prolapse Mx: nulliparous OR wants future childbearing?
back: Sling surgery (colpopexy); prerequisite: uterocervical length <12.5 cm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Prolapse Mx: reproductive-age female who does NOT want future childbearing?
back: Fothergill surgery / Manchester repair.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Prolapse Mx: 3rd-degree in a menopausal female (no cysto/rectocele)?
back: Vaginal hysterectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Prolapse Mx: 3rd-degree menopausal female WITH cystocele & rectocele?
back: Ward Mayo's hysterectomy = vaginal hysterectomy + anterior colporrhaphy (cystocele) + posterior colpoperineorrhaphy (rectocele).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Prolapse Mx: 55–60 yr female with prolapse + comorbidities?
back: Le Fort's colpocleisis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Prolapse Mx: elderly unfit / pregnant / immediate postpartum female?
back: Ring pessary.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Prolapse Mx: 1st/2nd-degree prolapse, and treatment of a decubitus ulcer?
back: 1st/2nd degree: Kegel's exercises. Decubitus ulcer (from venous congestion): acriflavine + glycerine packing.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Dribbling of urine ONLY on raised intra-abdominal pressure — diagnosis?
back: Stress urinary incontinence.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Continuous dribbling + NORMAL bladder emptying — which fistula?
back: Ureterovaginal fistula.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Continuous dribbling + NO bladder emptying — which fistula?
back: Vesicovaginal fistula (VVF).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Urine from urethra + vagina at the time of urination — which fistula?
back: Urethrovaginal fistula.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Most common type of fistula, and its commonest cause in developed vs developing countries?
back: VVF. Developed: total abdominal hysterectomy. Developing: obstructed labour.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: IOC and gold standard for VVF?
back: IOC: methylene blue 3-swab test. Gold standard: cystoscopy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Methylene-blue 3-swab test interpretation (upper / middle / lowermost)?
back: Upper swab wet + unstained → ureterovaginal fistula. Middle swab blue + wet → VVF. Lowermost swab blue + wet → urethrovaginal fistula.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Surgery of choice for VVF — vaginal vs abdominal route?
back: Vaginal: Latzko repair. Abdominal: Chassar Moir technique / O'Connor surgery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Post-op instructions after VVF repair (intercourse, pregnancy, catheter)?
back: No p/s, P/V, or intercourse for 3–6 months; no pregnancy for 1 year; catheter for 14 days.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Timing of VVF surgical repair based on when it is identified?
back: <24 hrs: immediate repair. >24 hrs: after 6 weeks–3 months.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Mode of delivery after conception following VVF repair?
back: Classical cesarean section.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: SUI surgery: most commonly done vs gold standard?
back: Most common: tension-free trans-obturator tape (TOT)/TVT — vaginal/day-care, suspends middle of urethra. Gold standard: Burch colposuspension (abdominal) — proximal urethra to Cooper's/iliopectineal ligament.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Drug useful in SUI?
back: Duloxetine.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Causes of cyclical hematuria?
back: Vesicouterine fistula; bladder endometriosis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Average age of puberty onset in females vs males?
back: Females 10.5 yrs; males 11.5 yrs.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: First sign of puberty in females vs males?
back: Females: growth spurt. Males: testicular enlargement.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Sequence of puberty in females (order the events).
back: Growth spurt → thelarche/breast budding (1st VISIBLE sign, Tanner 2) → pubarche → hair on mons pubis (Tanner 3) → peak height velocity → (6 months later) menarche (~12.5 yrs, within 2 yrs of thelarche).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Actions of estrogen during puberty?
back: Breast development, reproductive-tract (uterine) development, cervical mucus production, ↑ bone mass, ↑ clotting factors.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Main hormone for breast vs pubic/axillary hair development?
back: Breast: estrogen. Pubic & axillary hair: androgen.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Delayed puberty definition (females/males), commonest cause, and DOC?
back: Females: no thelarche by 13 yrs. Males: no testicular enlargement by 14 yrs. M/c cause: constitutional delay. DOC: pulsatile GnRH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Precocious puberty definition (females/males), and precocious menstruation?
back: Females: thelarche <8 yrs (m/c). Males: testicular enlargement <9 yrs. Precocious menstruation: menarche <10 yrs.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Central precocious puberty: mechanism, LH/FSH/E pattern, commonest & 2nd cause, Ix, DOC?
back: Premature HPO-axis activation; LH↑, FSH↑, E↑. M/c: idiopathic; 2nd: brain tumour (hamartoma). Ix: MRI brain. DOC: continuous GnRH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Peripheral precocious puberty: mechanism and LH/FSH/hormone pattern?
back: Exogenous/peripheral estrogen or androgen; LH↓, FSH↓, estrogen/androgen↑.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Causes of ISOSEXUAL vs HETEROSEXUAL peripheral precocious puberty?
back: Isosexual: hypothyroidism, estrogen-secreting ovarian tumour, McCune-Albright syndrome. Heterosexual: CAH, androgen-secreting ovarian/adrenal tumours.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Workup to do in ALL cases of precocious puberty?
back: Bone-age estimation; LH/FSH/estrogen (central vs peripheral); MRI brain (hamartoma); TSH (hypothyroidism); TVS (ovarian tumour).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Additional workup for HETEROSEXUAL precocious puberty?
back: Testosterone; DHEA-S (adrenal tumour); 17-OH progesterone (screen for CAH).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Neurotransmitters in puberty: dormancy, pulsatile GnRH, permissive role?
back: GABA keeps hypothalamus dormant; kisspeptin drives pulsatile GnRH; leptin has a permissive role.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: McCune-Albright syndrome triad?
back: Precocious puberty + polyostotic fibrous dysplasia + café-au-lait spots.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: First half of the menstrual cycle: name, main hormone, and what initiates the ovarian cycle?
back: Proliferative/follicular phase; main hormone estrogen; ovarian cycle initiated by FSH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Hormones produced by granulosa cells in the follicular phase?
back: Estrogen, inhibin B, and AMH (from small growing follicles).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Estrogen's feedback on FSH and on LH?
back: Negative feedback on FSH; negative on LH EXCEPT when estrogen ≥200 pg × 48 hrs → triggers LH surge.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: LH surge: what initiates it, what maintains it, and which cell does LH act on?
back: Initiated by estrogen (≥200 pg × 48 hrs); maintained by estrogen + progesterone; LH acts on the theca cell. Ovulation is due to the LH surge.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Aromatase converts what to what, and where are E1 vs E2 chiefly made?
back: Androgen → estrogen. E1 in adipocytes; E2 in granulosa cells.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: At ovulation, the primary oocyte converts to what, and arrested at which stage?
back: Secondary oocyte, arrested in metaphase of meiosis II; follicle → corpus luteum.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Time intervals: LH surge→ovulation, LH peak→ovulation, estrogen peak→ovulation?
back: LH surge→ovulation: 32–36 hrs. LH peak→ovulation: 10–12 hrs. Estrogen peak→ovulation: 12–24 hrs.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Second half of the cycle: name, main hormone, and luteal-phase length rule?
back: Secretory/luteal phase; main hormone progesterone; luteal phase fixed at 14 days, so ovulation occurs 14 days before the next menstruation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Progesterone feedback at low vs high concentration?
back: Low concentration: +ve feedback (on LH & FSH). High concentration: -ve feedback.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which hormone resumes meiosis I, and how long before ovulation?
back: LH; ~3 hrs before ovulation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: FSH and LH receptor locations (females vs males)?
back: FSH receptors: females—granulosa cells; males—Sertoli cells. LH receptors: females—granulosa + theca cells.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Corpus luteum: hormones secreted, and what maintains it (non-pregnant vs pregnant)?
back: Secretes progesterone, estrogen, inhibin A. Maintained by LH (non-pregnant) and hCG (pregnant).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Corpus luteum maximum size/activity day, and its lifespan?
back: Day 22 (~8 days after ovulation) — used as a test for ovulation; lifespan 10–12 days (non-pregnant).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Follicle stages: primordial vs primary granulosa cell appearance, and oocyte arrest stage in primordial?
back: Primordial: 1° oocyte arrested in diplotene of prophase, single layer of FLAT granulosa cells. Primary: single layer of CUBOIDAL granulosa cells.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Graafian follicle: size, corona radiata, cumulus oophorus, and oocyte type?
back: 18–20 mm just before ovulation; corona radiata surrounds oocyte; cumulus oophorus connects oocyte to granulosa; contains a 2° oocyte.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: All follicles contain a primary oocyte EXCEPT which one?
back: The Graafian follicle (contains a secondary oocyte).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Where is natural GnRH secreted and in what manner; where are LH/FSH made?
back: GnRH: arcuate nucleus of hypothalamus, pulsatile. LH/FSH: anterior pituitary in response to GnRH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Natural estrogens E1/E2/E3 — which predominates in menopause, reproductive age, and which is most specific in pregnancy?
back: E1 (estrone): m/c in menopausal females. E2 (estradiol): m/c in reproductive-age & pregnant females. E3 (estriol): most specific estrogen in pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Most common synthetic estrogen in OCP vs HRT?
back: OCP: ethinyl estradiol (EE). HRT: earlier conjugated equine estrogen (CEE), newer 17β-estradiol.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Rank estrogen potency.
back: EE > CEE > E2 > E1 > E3.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Name 3rd-generation progestins and their key advantage.
back: Desogestrel, norgestimate, gestodene — least androgenic side effects.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: 4th-generation progestins: characteristic and examples?
back: Anti-androgenic — cyproterone acetate, spironolactone, drospirenone, dienogest.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Most common progestin used in OCP, and ideal progestins for PCOS?
back: OCP: EE + 3rd-gen progesterone. PCOS: 3rd/4th-gen progesterone.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: HPO-axis defect at hypothalamus/pituitary — hormone pattern and name?
back: GnRH↓, LH↓, FSH↓, estrogen↓ = hypogonadotropic hypogonadism.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: HPO-axis defect at ovary/testes — hormone pattern and name?
back: LH↑, FSH↑, estrogen/androgen↓ = hypergonadotropic hypogonadism.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: HPO-axis defect at the uterus — hormone pattern and name?
back: LH normal, FSH normal, estrogen normal = normogonadotropism.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Two conditions with raised LH and normal FSH?
back: PCOS and androgen insensitivity syndrome (AIS).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Kallmann syndrome: gene, mechanism, hormone pattern, classic association?
back: KAL-1 gene defect → defective migration of GnRH neurons from olfactory epithelium; hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (GnRH/FSH/LH/sex steroids all↓); males>females; associated with anosmia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Kallmann syndrome: presentation and DOC?
back: Delayed puberty, 1° amenorrhea, infertility; DOC = pulsatile GnRH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cervical mucus under estrogen vs progesterone?
back: Estrogen: thin/watery/profuse, spinnbarkeit (elastic), ferning + from day 7, favours sperm entry. Progesterone: thick/viscous/scanty, non-elastic, ferning gone by day 17, unfavourable for sperm (basis of progesterone contraceptives).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Effect of estrogen vs progesterone on coagulation factors and salt/water?
back: Estrogen: ↑ clotting factors (so E+P contraceptives c/i with h/o thrombosis/MI) and salt/water retention. Progesterone: no effect on clotting; causes salt/water wasting.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Effect of estrogen vs progesterone on endometrial cancer risk?
back: Estrogen ↑ risk; progesterone ↓ risk.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Other estrogen effects (cell, BMD, lipids) and progesterone effects (cancer, temperature)?
back: Estrogen: predominant superficial cells, ↑BMD, cardioprotective (↓LDL, ↑HDL). Progesterone: ↑breast cancer risk, ↑basal body temperature.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Normal vs low FSH values, and best day to test LH/FSH?
back: Normal FSH 4–10 IU; low <4 IU (1–3). Best time: day 2–4 of cycle.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: ↑LH + normal FSH: which conditions in primary vs secondary amenorrhea, and how differentiated?
back: Primary amenorrhea: AIS. Secondary amenorrhea: PCOS. Differentiated by karyotyping.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: ↑LH AND ↑FSH with amenorrhea points to what?
back: A gonadal disorder (ovary/testes).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Effect of prolactin on GnRH?
back: Negative feedback → ↓LH, ↓FSH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Hormone profile (LH/FSH/E): Asherman vs Turner vs Kallmann/Sheehan vs PCOS/AIS?
back: Asherman: all normal. Turner: LH↑, FSH↑, E↓. Kallmann/Sheehan: LH↓, FSH↓, E↓ (Kallmann also GnRH↓). PCOS/AIS: LH↑, FSH normal, androgens↑↑.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When is a triple-layered endometrium seen?
back: Late proliferative phase > ovulation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: GnRH analogue: route, examples, and effect when given pulsatile vs continuous?
back: Orally inactive (SC/intranasal); e.g. leuprolide, buserelin. Pulsatile → ↑LH/FSH/E; continuous → ↓LH/FSH/E.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Uses of PULSATILE GnRH vs CONTINUOUS GnRH/GnRH antagonist?
back: Pulsatile: delayed puberty, Kallmann, anovulation. Continuous/antagonist: precocious puberty, fibroid, endometriosis, ER+ breast cancer, hirsutism, prostate cancer.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: GnRH antagonist: route, example, effect?
back: Orally active (e.g. cetrorelix), expensive; ↓LH/FSH/E.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: hMG: source, content, uses, and disadvantages?
back: Source: urine of post-menopausal women; 75 IU each of LH & FSH; used in IVF and anovulation; disadvantages: OHSS (highest risk), multifetal pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: DOC for anovulation: general, in PCOS, with pituitary ablation, and in Kallmann?
back: General: clomiphene citrate. PCOS: letrozole > clomiphene (both need a normal HPO axis). Pituitary ablation: hMG. Kallmann: pulsatile GnRH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Normal menstrual cycle: length and bleeding (days/volume)?
back: Length: average 28 days, normal 24–38. Bleeding: 2–8 days (~4.5–5), volume ~80 mL (35–50 typical).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define hypomenorrhea and menorrhagia, with a classic cause of each.
back: Hypomenorrhea: <2 days / <20 mL (Asherman). Menorrhagia: >80 mL / >8 days (fibroid/adenomyosis).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define polymenorrhea, oligomenorrhea, and metrorrhagia.
back: Polymenorrhea: short cycles. Oligomenorrhea: long cycles / <9 cycles per year. Metrorrhagia: irregular/intermenstrual bleeding (polyp).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Causes of AUB — recall the PALM-COEIN structure.
back: Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia, Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic (IUCD), Not classified.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Investigations for AUB: 1st, 2nd, and when biopsy?
back: 1st: UPT (except menopausal). 2nd: TVS (c/i in pubertal females). If indicated: endometrial biopsy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: First-line drugs for AUB?
back: Tranexamic acid, OCPs, progesterone (oral or Mirena).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Endometrial biopsy: instruments (India vs worldwide) and timing?
back: India: Karman's cannula. Worldwide: Pipelle/Vabra aspirator. Timing: day 26–27 of cycle; done in OPD.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Endometrial-biopsy thickness thresholds: reproductive, perimenopausal (India/worldwide), postmenopausal?
back: Reproductive: ≥11 mm or persistent bleeding. Perimenopausal: India ≥40 yrs / worldwide ≥45 yrs regardless of USG. Postmenopausal: ≥4 mm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Endometrial histology: proliferative vs early-secretory(ovulation) vs late-secretory features?
back: Proliferative: simple tubular glands, telescoping, pseudostratification. Early secretory/ovulation: subnuclear vacuoles (1st sign of ovulation, day 17–18). Late secretory: corkscrew/sawtooth glands, progesterone-driven secretion.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: IOC and gold standard for endometrial pathology?
back: IOC: endometrial biopsy (best 2–3 days before menses). Gold standard: hysteroscopy + fractional curettage > hysteroscopy + D&C > fractional curettage > hysteroscopy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Indications for fractional curettage?
back: Hyperplasia with atypia; insufficient sample; hyperplasia without atypia + persistent bleeding despite Rx; cervical stenosis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: AUB in adolescent girls: cause, investigations, contraindications, DOC?
back: Cause: anovulation > coagulopathy. Ix: UPT, serum iron, coagulation profile. C/I: P/V exam, endometrial biopsy. DOC: OCPs.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Metropathia hemorrhagica: what is it and how does it present?
back: Anovulatory bleeding in perimenopausal females; presents as 2–3 months of amenorrhea followed by heavy bleeding.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: ≥40 yr with heavy menstrual bleeding: initial three investigations?
back: UPT, TVS, endometrial biopsy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Swiss-cheese pattern on biopsy: meaning, malignancy risk, and DOC?
back: Anovulatory bleeding due to perimenopause (metropathia hemorrhagica / Schroeder's disease); malignancy ~1%; DOC progesterone.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Endometrial hyperplasia malignancy risk: without atypia vs with atypia?
back: Without atypia: 1–3%. With atypia: 8–30%.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Management of endometrial hyperplasia WITHOUT atypia?
back: Progesterone — best: Mirena; 2nd best: continuous oral progesterone > cyclical (12–14 days). If bleeding/hyperplasia persists after 6 months → hysteroscopy + fractional curettage.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Management of endometrial hyperplasia WITH atypia?
back: Hysteroscopy + fractional curettage; best management = TAH / simple hysterectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Progesterone Rx duration for withdrawal bleeding vs endometrial protection?
back: Withdrawal bleeding: 4–6 days. Protective (anti-proliferative) effect: minimum 12–14 days.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Most common type, most malignant types, most common age of endometrial cancer?
back: M/c: adenocarcinoma. Most malignant: clear cell & papillary serous. Age 50–70 yrs (mean 60).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Most common vs most specific complaint, and most common route of spread of endometrial cancer?
back: M/c complaint: irregular bleeding (AUB-M). Most specific: postmenopausal bleeding. Spread: direct > lymphatic.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Familial syndromes raising endometrial cancer risk?
back: Lynch syndrome, BRCA1/2 mutations, Cowden syndrome (PTEN mutation on Chr 10).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Non-familial risk factors for endometrial cancer (HTN, obesity...)?
back: Hypertension, obesity, late menopause/early menarche, diabetes, atypical hyperplasia, unopposed estrogen (PCOS/HRT/granulosa cell tumour), nulliparity, tamoxifen.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Gatekeeper gene for endometrial cancer; Type 1 vs Type 2 features?
back: Gatekeeper gene: PTEN. Type 1 (adeno grade 1–2): good prognosis, a/w excess estrogen. Type 2 (adeno grade 3, papillary serous, clear cell): poor prognosis, NOT estrogen-associated.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Endometrial cancer staging: Stage 1A vs 1B, Stage 2, Stage 3 subtypes?
back: Stage 1: confined to uterus (1A <50% myometrium, 1B ≥50%). Stage 2: cervical involvement. Stage 3: 3A serosa/adnexa, 3B vagina/parametrium, 3C LN (C1 pelvic, C2 para-aortic).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Superficial inguinal LN involvement in endometrial/ovarian/cervical cancer = which stage?
back: Stage 4B (metastasis).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Endometrial cancer surgery by stage?
back: Stage 1: TAH + BSO. Stage 2/3: Wertheim's hysterectomy. Stage 4: debulking surgery. Staging by frozen section (surgical staging).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: LN dissection rules in endometrial cancer (and the stage IA exception)?
back: Pelvic & para-aortic LND in all stages; pre-op MRI for myometrial involvement. Stage IA exception: tumour <2 cm → no LND; ≥2 cm → pelvic LND.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Post-op therapy by risk group in endometrial cancer?
back: Low risk (adeno + grade 1/2 + stage IA): none. High risk (adeno stage 3/4, papillary serous, clear cell): chemo + radiotherapy. Intermediate (rest): radiotherapy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Prognostic factors associated with GOOD outcome in endometrial cancer?
back: ER-positive and PR-positive tumours.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Causes of postmenopausal bleeding: worldwide vs India?
back: Worldwide: polyp > endometrial atrophy. India: cancer cervix.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Postmenopausal bleeding management algorithm by endometrial thickness on TVS?
back: <4 mm: tranexamic acid (if persistent → hysteroscopy + fractional curettage). ≥4 mm: endometrial biopsy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Core pathophysiology of PCOS, and normal vs PCOS androgen levels?
back: Excessive ovarian androgen. Normal androgen <70 ng; PCOS 70–200 ng. ↑Androgen is folliculotoxic → no follicle matures → no ovulation → anovulatory infertility, plus hirsutism.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Rotterdam criteria for PCOS?
back: Any 2 of 3: (1) hyperandrogenism (hirsutism / ↑testosterone), (2) ovulatory dysfunction (oligo-/amenorrhea), (3) polycystic ovaries on USG (≥12 follicles 2–9 mm, or volume >10 cc). European criteria: ≥20 follicles.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Features NOT part of the Rotterdam criteria?
back: Insulin resistance, necklace pattern, obesity, LH:FSH ratio, menometrorrhagia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In obese PCOS: E1:E2 ratio, and the LH:FSH pattern (vs normal)?
back: Obese PCOS E1:E2 = 2:1 (normal 1:2). Normal LH:FSH = 1:1; in PCOS 2:1 or 3:1 (LH↑, FSH normal); tested on D2–D3.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: HAIR-AN syndrome triad?
back: Hyperandrogenism, insulin resistance, acanthosis nigricans.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: PCOS lab findings — what is increased, decreased, unchanged?
back: ↑: testosterone, DHEA-S (adrenal), LH, LDL, estrogen (if obese), AMH. ↓: progesterone, SHBG (due to insulin & androgens). Unchanged: FSH, prolactin. Progesterone challenge test +ve.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Distinguishing PCOS from late-onset CAH — screening and diagnostic tests?
back: Screening: 17-OH progesterone (<200 rules out CAH; 200–800 intermediate; ≥800 confirms CAH). Diagnostic: ACTH stimulation test.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Chromosomes and loci associated with PCOS?
back: Chromosomes 2 & 9; loci THADA and DENND1A.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: General PCOS management principles and DOCs (cycles/hirsutism/insulin resistance)?
back: First step: lifestyle modification; treat only presenting complaints. DOC irregular cycles: OCP (3rd/4th-gen progesterone). DOC hirsutism: OCP. DOC insulin resistance: metformin. Avoid spironolactone if she wants to conceive.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: PCOS infertility management — first step and 1st/2nd/3rd-line drugs?
back: First step: lifestyle + folic acid 400 mcg (5–10% ovulate). 1st line: letrozole > clomiphene. 2nd line: hMG, CC+metformin, CC+corticosteroid (↑DHEAS), OCP×3 cycles then CC. 3rd line: pulsatile GnRH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Outdated surgery for PCOS infertility — mechanism and side effect?
back: Laparoscopic ovarian drilling — destroys ovarian stroma → theca cell destruction → ↓androgen; side effect: premature ovarian insufficiency.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Long-term PCOS complications by mechanism, and the complication NOT seen?
back: ↑Androgen: dyslipidemia (↑LDL, ↓HDL), CHD. ↑Estrogen: endometrial Ca, endometrial hyperplasia, ovarian Ca±, NASH. Obesity: anxiety/depression, sleep apnea, metabolic syndrome. NOT seen: osteoporosis (estrogen normal/↑).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Commonest cause of hirsutism in young females vs commonest cause of rapid-onset hirsutism?
back: Young females: PCOS. Rapid onset: androgen-secreting tumour.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Hirsutism workup: testosterone interpretation (normal / 70–200 / ≥200 ng)?
back: Normal: idiopathic hirsutism. 70–200 ng: PCOS / late-onset CAH. ≥200 ng: androgen-secreting tumour of ovary/adrenal.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Main sources of androgen in females (proportions) and androgen receptor location?
back: Ovary 25%, adrenal 25%, peripheral conversion from androstenedione 50%. Androgen receptor on long arm of X chromosome.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Androgen >70 but <200 vs ≥200 ng — clinical effect; features of virilization?
back: >70 but <200: only hirsutism. ≥200: virilization — hirsutism, clitoromegaly, breast atrophy, voice deepening, ↑muscle mass.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Hirsutism scoring system and threshold?
back: Ferriman-Gallwey score (≥8).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Hirsutism treatment ladder?
back: DOC: OCP for 6 months. No relief: antiandrogens + OCP (most common spironolactone + OCP; others finasteride, flutamide, ketoconazole). Last resort: continuous GnRH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Topical drug for hirsutism, the drug that causes hirsutism, and why spironolactone is avoided when conceiving?
back: Topical: eflornithine. Causes hirsutism: danazol. Spironolactone avoided in those wanting to conceive — risk of ambiguous genitalia in a male fetus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: OHSS: commonest causative drug, drug NOT causing it, triggering factor, and mediator?
back: Commonest cause: hMG. Not causing: letrozole. Trigger: inj. hCG. Mediator: VEGF (makes capillaries leaky).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Inj. hCG: function, when to give, and when NOT to give?
back: Functions like LH to trigger ovulation. Give when follicles ≥2 and ≥18 mm. Do NOT give if follicles ≥20 and/or estradiol >3500 pg/mL (~200 pg E2 per follicle).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Early vs late onset OHSS timing?
back: Early: <9 days after hCG. Late: ≥9 days after hCG (due to pregnancy).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: OHSS classification (mild/moderate/severe/critical)?
back: Mild: abdominal discomfort, USG B/L enlarged ovaries. Moderate: ascites on USG. Severe: clinical ascites. Critical: end-organ damage from thrombosis (ARDS, DIC, ARF).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When is OHSS admitted, and drugs used to reduce severity?
back: Admit if severe/critical/pregnant. Drugs: bromocriptine (prevents VEGF), IV albumin (volume expansion), heparin (thromboprophylaxis).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Ways to PREVENT OHSS?
back: Withhold inj. hCG; replace hCG with a GnRH agonist; in PCOS use frozen embryo transfer or metformin pretreatment.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Average age of menopause worldwide vs India; definitions of POI and late menopause?
back: Worldwide 51 yrs; India 47 yrs. POI: <40 yrs. Late menopause: >55 yrs.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Diagnostic criteria for menopause vs POI (incl. FSH values)?
back: Menopause: 12 months of amenorrhea. POI: raised FSH on 2 occasions 1 month apart, ≥25 IU/L (earlier 40); normal FSH 1–10.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Hormonal changes in menopause (up vs down)?
back: ↓ AMH, inhibin, E2. ↑ vaginal pH, FSH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Hot flashes: most common symptom type, mechanism, neurotransmitters, timing?
back: M/c (vasomotor) symptom; due to ↓estrogen resetting the hypothalamic thermostat; serotonin–norepinephrine imbalance; worse at night (night sweats).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Osteoporosis in menopause: type of defect, IOC, DOC, and drugs acting on osteoblasts?
back: Quantitative bone defect (estrogen normally ↑ bone mass). IOC: DEXA scan. DOC: bisphosphonates (alendronate, pamidronate). Osteoblast-acting: strontium, teriparatide.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Estrogen and CAD risk around menopause?
back: Estrogen is cardioprotective (↑HDL, ↓LDL); CAD risk rises after menopause but HRT is NOT given for prevention; in women >60 HRT slightly increases CAD risk; E+P HRT increases risk more than E-only.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause / senile vaginitis: cause, symptoms, management?
back: Due to ↓estrogen; vaginal dryness, spotting, dyspareunia. Mx: 1st line topical vaginal lubricants; 2nd line topical estrogen cream.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: HRT: only indication, E vs E+P choice, commonest estrogen, contraceptive status, VTE alternative?
back: Only indication: severe hot flashes. Uterus present → E+P; uterus absent → E only. Commonest estrogen: 17β-estradiol. Not a contraceptive. With h/o VTE: estrogen not given, DOC fluoxetine (SSRI).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Contraindications to HRT?
back: H/o breast cancer; h/o CAD/stroke/VTE; smokers; severe hypertriglyceridemia; undiagnosed vaginal bleeding; severe uncontrolled HTN; diabetes with vasculopathy; migraine with aura.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Estrogen-only HRT: advantages and disadvantages?
back: Advantages: ↓hot flashes, improves osteoporosis, ↓colon Ca. Disadvantages: ↑endometrial Ca, ↑clotting factors (VTE), ovarian Ca±.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: E+P HRT: advantages and disadvantages (vs E-only)?
back: Advantages: ↓hot flashes, improves osteoporosis, ↓colorectal Ca, ↓endometrial Ca. Disadvantages: ↑clotting, ovarian Ca±, ↑breast Ca.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: HRT duration limits, and what it does NOT change?
back: Max 5 years; for perimenopausal/Turner up to age 51. Does NOT increase cervical cancer; does NOT decrease CAD risk.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: AMH: normal value, source, and levels in POI vs PCOS?
back: Normal 1–3 ng; secreted by granulosa cells of small growing (preantral & antral) follicles. POI: ↓ (<0.5 ng). PCOS: ↑ (>3 ng).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: POI: infertility Rx, metabolic cause, clinical/hormonal picture, chromosomal associations, libido Rx?
back: Infertility: donor egg + IVF. Metabolic cause: galactosemia. Clinical: 2° amenorrhea + ↑FSH (hypergonadotropic hypogonadism). Chromosomal: Turner mosaic, fragile X. ↓Libido: testosterone.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: SERM uses: hot flashes / vaginal dryness / anovulation / osteoporosis?
back: Hot flashes (from tamoxifen/clomiphene/raloxifene): E + bazedoxifene. Vaginal dryness: ospemifene. Anovulation: clomiphene (ovulation induction). Osteoporosis: raloxifene (s/e hot flashes).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Follicle numbers: maximum, at birth, at puberty, ovulated in lifetime, atresia per month?
back: Maximum 6–7 million at 5th month IUL; 1–2 million at birth; 4–5 lakh at puberty; ~500 ovulated in lifetime; ~1000 undergo atresia each month.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Asherman syndrome: pathology, commonest cause, and maximum-risk situations?
back: Intrauterine adhesions + thin defective endometrium. M/c cause: excessive curettage. Max risk: postpartum curettage, genital TB.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Asherman: commonest symptom group and single commonest symptom; 1st Ix and IOC?
back: Group: menstrual symptoms. Single: infertility > 2° amenorrhea > hypomenorrhea. 1st Ix: HSG. IOC: hysteroscopy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: HSG appearance of Asherman vs a polyp/fibroid?
back: Asherman: multiple filling defects, irregular 'moth-eaten' outline. Polyp/fibroid: single, smooth, regular outline.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Asherman hormone profile and challenge tests?
back: LH, FSH, estrogen, progesterone all normal; progesterone challenge test -ve; E+P challenge test -ve.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Management of Asherman syndrome?
back: Hysteroscopic resection of adhesions + insert paediatric Foley's catheter + E+P for 3 months.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Definition of secondary amenorrhea, and its commonest cause?
back: Absence of menstruation ≥3 months / 90 days in a previously menstruating female. M/c cause: pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Initial investigations for secondary amenorrhea?
back: UPT (rule out pregnancy), TSH, prolactin, LH/E2/FSH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Secondary amenorrhea with FSH DECREASED (1–3 IU): causes?
back: Hypothalamic (excessive exercise, bulimia, anorexia, stress) or pituitary (Sheehan's, prolactinoma).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Secondary amenorrhea: FSH increased (>10) vs FSH normal (4–10) — interpretation?
back: FSH >10: ovarian (POI). FSH normal: PCOS (progesterone challenge +ve, LH↑) or Asherman (progesterone -ve, E+P -ve, LH normal).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Symptom clues: PPH + failure to lactate; headache/visual disturbance/galactorrhea?
back: PPH + failure to lactate → Sheehan's syndrome (necrosis of anterior pituitary). Headache + visual disturbance + galactorrhea → prolactin-secreting pituitary tumour.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Amenorrhea + ↓FSH: MRI findings interpretation (empty sella / SOL / normal)?
back: Empty sella → Sheehan's. Space-occupying mass → prolactinoma. Normal → hypothalamic cause.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Serum prolactin cut-offs and next steps?
back: Normal up to 20–25 ng. Mild <50 ng: repeat. >50 ng: MRI. DOC hyperprolactinemia: cabergoline; in infertile females: bromocriptine.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Menstrual effects of hypothyroidism vs hyperthyroidism?
back: Hypothyroidism: ↑ bleeding. Hyperthyroidism: amenorrhea/oligomenorrhea.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Sheehan's vs prolactinoma: commonest and 2nd commonest symptoms?
back: Sheehan's: m/c failure to lactate, 2nd 2° amenorrhea. Prolactinoma: m/c 2° amenorrhea, 2nd infertility.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Pituitary apoplexy: setting, postpartum features, and treatment?
back: Pituitary adenoma + PPH; postpartum shock, hypoglycemia, headache, visual disturbance; Rx corticosteroids.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Streak ovary vs dysgenetic testis — which syndromes, and malignancy/management?
back: Streak ovary: Turner's syndrome — no malignancy risk, should NOT be removed. Dysgenetic testis: Swyer's syndrome — ↑malignancy → gonadectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Swyer's syndrome causes: idiopathic vs genetic split?
back: 90% idiopathic; 5–10% due to a point mutation in the SRY gene.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Ovotestis (true hermaphrodite): genotype, gonads, internal vs external genitalia, sex assigned?
back: Genotype 46XX > 46XY; both ovary + testis in one individual. Internal genitalia: female on ovary side, male on testis side. External: ambiguous (resembles male). Usually assigned male at birth; may show gynecomastia (ovarian estrogen), menstruation, infertility.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: 46XY with FEMALE external genitalia — three causes?
back: Complete androgen insensitivity, Swyer's syndrome, 5α-reductase deficiency.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: 46XY with AMBIGUOUS external genitalia — causes?
back: Partial AIS; CAH due to 17,20-lyase deficiency or 17α-hydroxylase deficiency.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Enzyme deficiencies causing ambiguous genitalia in BOTH XX & XY?
back: p450-OR deficiency and 3β-HSD deficiency.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: 46XX with ambiguous genitalia — causes?
back: CAH and aromatase deficiency.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Ambiguous genitalia: commonest cause in males vs females, and first step in workup?
back: Males: partial androgen insensitivity syndrome. Females: CAH. First step: physical exam — palpable gonads (testis) → XY; impalpable (ovary) → XX. IOC: karyotyping.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Methods of sex determination: most common, best, and the Barr body rule with false +ve/–ve?
back: M/c: external genitalia. Best: karyotyping. Barr bodies = (number of X chromosomes) − 1 (normal male 0, female 1). False +ve: Klinefelter (male with Barr body). False −ve: Turner (female without Barr body).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: CAH is the commonest cause of what, and the commonest & 2nd enzyme deficiency?
back: Commonest cause of ambiguous genitalia in females / female pseudohermaphrodite. M/c enzyme: 21α-hydroxylase; 2nd: 11β-hydroxylase.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: 21α-/11β-hydroxylase deficiency: effects of low aldosterone and low cortisol?
back: ↓Aldosterone: hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, salt-water wasting, metabolic acidosis, hypotension. ↓Cortisol: hypoglycemia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In 21α-/11β-deficiency, which hormone rises and what is the genital effect (♀ vs ♂)?
back: ↑ACTH → ↑androgen. Females: ambiguous genitalia. Males: normal male genitalia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: CAH screening vs diagnostic test (with 17-OHP cut-offs)?
back: Screening: 17-OH progesterone (<200 rules out, 200–800 intermediate, ≥800 confirms). Diagnostic: ACTH stimulation test.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: CAH management steps?
back: Begin Mx without waiting for karyotype. 1) DOC corticosteroids (-ve feedback on ACTH → adrenal not stimulated → ↓androgen). 2) Correct fluid-electrolyte imbalance. 3) Later, surgically correct clitoromegaly.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Aromatase deficiency: mechanism, presentation, and 17-OHP level?
back: Maternal androgens not converted to estrogen → ambiguous genitalia in XX fetus + hirsutism in mother during pregnancy; 17-OH progesterone normal.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: p450-OR deficiency association?
back: Skeletal anomalies (e.g. craniosynostosis).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: 46XY DSD with female external genitalia: typical presentation and findings suggestive of XY?
back: Assigned female at birth, c/o primary amenorrhea at puberty. XY clues: B/L inguinal hernia/mass (undescended testis), ↑androgen, palpable gonads.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Complete AIS: gonad, defect, uterus, breast, hair, and LH/FSH/testosterone?
back: Testis normal but insensitive to testosterone & DHT; AMH present → both ducts regress → uterus absent; external female; breast well developed (testosterone→aromatase→estrogen); scanty pubic/axillary hair. LH↑, FSH normal, testosterone↑.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Swyer syndrome: gonad, ducts, uterus, breast, and LH/FSH/testosterone?
back: Dysgenetic testis (no testosterone, no AMH) → Wolffian regresses, Müllerian grows → uterus present; external female; breast absent. LH↑, FSH↑, testosterone↓.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: 5α-reductase deficiency: ducts, uterus, external genitalia at birth vs puberty, hormones?
back: Testosterone present but no DHT; Wolffian grows, Müllerian regresses → uterus absent; female external genitalia at birth, virilization at puberty (clitoromegaly, hirsutism, ↑muscle mass, voice deepening, scanty hair). LH/FSH/testosterone normal.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: AIS management: gender care, gonadectomy timing, HRT, vaginoplasty?
back: Female gender-affirming care; gonadectomy at 14–16 yrs (after breast development, due to malignancy risk); E HRT till ~51 yrs; vaginoplasty (McIndoe or laparoscopic Vecchietti) just before/after marriage.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Swyer syndrome management?
back: Female gender-affirming care; gonadectomy immediately after diagnosis (malignancy risk); estrogen ×1 yr (breast development) then lifelong E+P (uterus present).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Partial AIS (Reifenstein) vs 5α-reductase deficiency — breasts, pubic hair, onset of clitoromegaly?
back: Both: 46XY female with 1° amenorrhea, B/L inguinal hernia, ambiguous genitalia. Partial AIS: breasts well developed, scanty pubic hair, clitoromegaly at birth. 5α-reductase: breasts absent, scanty pubic hair, clitoromegaly at puberty.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Definition of primary amenorrhea (three criteria)?
back: No menses by 13 yrs without breast budding (Tanner 1); OR no menses by 15 yrs with breast budding (Tanner ≥2); OR no menses within 3 yrs of thelarche.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Primary amenorrhea causes WITH uterus vs WITHOUT uterus?
back: With uterus: Kallmann, gonadal dysgenesis (Turner 45XO, Swyer 46XY, pure gonadal dysgenesis 46XX), cryptomenorrhea. Without uterus: Müllerian agenesis, complete AIS.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Primary amenorrhea by hormones: ↓FSH↓LH↓E vs ↑LH↑FSH↓E?
back: ↓FSH↓LH↓E: hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (Kallmann). ↑LH↑FSH↓E: hypergonadotropic hypogonadism (Turner, Swyer = gonadal dysgenesis).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Primary amenorrhea: normal FSH with ↑LH vs normal FSH with normal LH?
back: Normal FSH + ↑LH: AIS. Normal FSH + normal LH: Müllerian agenesis (uterus absent) or cryptomenorrhea (uterus present, bulky due to hematometra).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Workup algorithm for primary amenorrhea?
back: UPT → physical exam (breast, pubic hair, height, palpable gonads) → pelvic USG. Uterus present → LH/FSH/estrogen. Uterus absent → karyotyping (IOC).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cryptomenorrhea: meaning and clinical features?
back: Hidden menstruation; cyclical monthly abdominal pain; breast development present.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Imperforate hymen vs transverse vaginal septum — local exam, cough impulse, PR exam, IOC?
back: Imperforate hymen: tense bulging hymen ± bluish, cough impulse +, hematocolpos more common, PR bulge on pulp of finger. Transverse septum: normal hymen, no cough impulse, hematometra/endometriosis more common, PR bulge on tip; IOC = MRI.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Management of imperforate hymen, and the types of hymen (A–E)?
back: Cruciate incision. Types: A multiparous/sexually active, B imperforate, C virgin, D cribriform, E septate.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Kallmann syndrome clinical features, gene, commonest karyotype, management?
back: Female 1° amenorrhea WITH uterus present; breast & pubic hair absent; low FSH & LH; anosmia. Gene: KAL-1. M/c: 46XY > 46XX. Mx: pulsatile GnRH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Turner's karyotype and the mosaic variant gonad pattern?
back: 45XO; mosaic 45XO/46XY — one side streak ovary, other dysgenetic testes.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Turner's malignancy risk and gonadal tumours?
back: Streak ovaries: no malignancy. Dysgenetic/undescended testes: ↑malignancy → gonadectomy. M/c gonadal tumour: gonadoblastoma; m/c malignancy: dysgerminoma.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Associated features of Turner's syndrome?
back: Short stature (SHOX mutation), neck webbing, low posterior hairline, shield chest, widely spaced nipples, SNHL, autoimmune disease (diabetes, Hashimoto's, celiac), cubitus valgus, heart disease (bicuspid aortic valve > coarctation of aorta).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Turner's investigations beyond karyotyping?
back: ECHO; renal USG (horseshoe kidney); TSH/T3/T4 (Hashimoto's); CBC, lipid profile, fasting glucose; RFT/LFT; audiometry (SNHL); anti-endomysial antibodies (celiac).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Turner's management?
back: Growth hormone for short stature (≤8 yrs); estrogen ×1 yr (breast development) then E+P lifelong (up to ~51 yrs).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Turner vs Swyer: karyotype, gonads, associated features, stature, gonadectomy?
back: Both: uterus present, breast & pubic hair absent. Turner: 45XO, streak ovary, features present, short stature, gonadectomy only in mosaic. Swyer: 46XY, dysgenetic testis, no features, normal stature, gonadectomy required.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: MRKH (Müllerian agenesis) vs AIS — karyotype, gonad, breast, pubic hair, testosterone, LH?
back: MRKH: 46XX, ovary, breast present, pubic hair present, normal ♀ testosterone, LH normal. AIS: 46XY, testes, breast well developed (Tanner 4/5), pubic hair scanty, male testosterone, LH↑. Both: female external genitalia, uterus absent, FSH normal.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: MRKH management (vaginoplasty & infertility) and associations?
back: Vaginoplasty just before/after marriage; infertility via IVF then surrogacy. Associated with renal and skeletal anomalies.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Distinctive features of AIS (vs MRKH)?
back: B/L inguinal hernia; worst reproductive outcome.
